All right. Good evening, everyone. Thank you for joining us. A little housekeeping real fast. If you're in the chat there, just give me a thumbs up or type in okay if you can hear me okay. And if you can see my screen. Excellent. Thank you very much. Well, on behalf of the Chaldeany Institute, I want to thank you for coming to our influence challenge webinar, hosted by the Godfather of influence himself, Dr. Robert Chaldeany. My name is Dr. Chris Phelps. Before we get to started tonight, I want to start it off by just giving you a little bit of my story and a testimonial about how Dr. Chaldeany has had a direct impact on my life and my businesses, much as I'm hoping you him and his research will have an impact on you and your businesses. So for those of you that don't know me, I am a Chaldeany method certified trainer, which means that I got to train under Dr. Chaldeany directly. But in addition to being a Chaldeany method certified trainer, I also happen to be a pretty good dentist. So I graduated dental school in 2003, and I came back to my home in Charlotte, North Carolina, where I reside today. And I joined my wife's dentist who had been her dentist since she was 17. As a kind of an apprenticeship kind of program as most dentists do when they get out of school to learn under an older mentor. And what was interesting is my partner, Dr. Grimes, who I had joined had this nice little established practice and historic part of Charlotte. A little old house that he had renovated into a dental office, working four days a week and you know really wasn't working too hard and just he had a nice life. And what was nice about Dr. Grimes was he really, you know, encouraged me to get out there and help find new marketing channels for the business, help us grow. And before he knew it, we had gone from that one little old house and a tiny historic part of Charlotte to building for what we call fee for service dental offices. So four different locations. We had over 100 team members working for us, significantly increasing the revenue of our practices over that seven year period. But what was interesting was is to kind of like in my mindset, getting out of dental school, it's growth for growth's sake, right? Just by a bill more practices and more practices and more practices. But I realized as I started getting into it about year seven of my career that there was a problem there. Because I was stuck between two mindsets, one where I was still doing clinical dentistry, you know, five, six days a week sometimes. But at the same time, running the operations for the practices, running the marketing, making all the business decisions, managing the team, etc. And so I was kind of stuck between these two worlds. And I realized that that couldn't go on much longer. So I had to do something bold. And I decided to simplify my life. So to change my mindset from growing for growth's sake, okay, to maximizing the capacity of the offices that I had left. So I decided to sell my two best offices to some of my partners. So the two highest revenue producing least amount of debt, most profitable practices. And me and my regional partner took over the two worst practices. So highest debt, least amount of profit, so to speak. And so going from four to two, you know, what I noticed was when you take over your two worst practices, that's not necessarily a good thing. The reason they're called the worst. So I had all these little fires that were going on in behavioral issues with my patients, not doing the treatment recommendations that myself and my associate doctors were recommending, not referring to the practice, not doing reviews. I had issues with my team and team management, you know, why was it that I had to keep telling my team to get stuff done versus why wouldn't they just do their job and get stuff done. Right. Why was it that when the cat was away, the mice would play. So all of these little fires going on in my practices and I couldn't put my finger on the root cause of that problem. Okay, it could put my finger on the root cause. And so that was when I was fortunate that a friend asked me to come here at a keynote presentation. And lo and behold, who was the keynote speaking was none other than the godfather himself, Dr. Robert Chaldini. And Dr. Chaldini talked about the six principles of persuasion that he had done his research on and proven existed. There was one of them in particular that was this light bulb for me that, wow, that one right there. That guy is the root cause behind the majority of the problems in my practice. Okay, cool. All right, well, this guy obviously is an expert. He knows what he's talking about. I need to learn from the best. And so that's why I sought him out and followed up my training with him for over a year. But in doing so, what I was able to do was to go back and use my two dental practices, the struggling ones as my laboratory. And I went back and proactively started looking at how can I change things? How can I get more of a commitment? Because that was really the principle and question here, the consistency principle. How could I get more of a commitment out of my patients, a commitment out of my doctors and more of a commitment out of my team? How could I create some influence systems at each of the touch points that my practice was struggling in? I had to make written commitments to myself, right, to get a better commitment for myself to work on marketing leadership and communication with those patients. And ultimately working in a mastermind type group setting where I was making public commitments to others who were going to help keep me accountable for these goals and these systems that I had created. What was the end result? Well, as you can imagine, you know, when I took these behavioral science research studies and then went and used them, my practice as my laboratory to experiment in, what we saw were the same kind of results. And that's what really drew me to this, you know, this is not our anecdotal opinion. This is not our guess that this might work for you and your situation. This is everything that we're going to talk about tonight, even though we may not have time to go into all the studies behind it, is based in lots of behavioral science research and what people are really using to make the majority of their decisions. Right. And so I realized early on, I need to get out of making my strategies around the excuses people give me after I'd hear a no. In my world, that was you're too expensive and my insurance doesn't cover it. And instead I had to back it up and realize that I need to make my strategies on these other things, the things people are actually using to make the majority of their decisions. And what I found when I did that, and I made my strategies around these principles that were naturally and just organically present in my conversations and interactions with the patients, suddenly their behavior changed. And within a three year period of time, I had those two struggling practices doing more revenue, being more profitable and allowing myself to have more free time than when I had the four offices as well. And so to say Dr. Chaldini has a huge impact on my life is an understatement. I will personally forever be grateful to him for that. So I hope some of you guys can have that same kind of experience like I did when you hear these things for the first time and you see the potential for the application in your practice, and you can go back and do that. And this is one of the main reasons why I felt called to join the Chaldini Institute. Okay, this is the legacy project Dr. Chaldini has built to spread the message of influence. Okay, and the ethical use of his principles to the masses. Okay, we all know Dr. Chaldini has a great name and he's trusted by many organizations that all of you have heard. They did a recent study a couple years ago, interviewing top, the top 100 CEOs and entrepreneurs out there, many of which are very famous people as you can see here and they asked them all the simple questions. What are your top 10 business books that you recommend for all entrepreneurs and business owners out there. And the number one consensus among them all, which book did they all say was the top. Dr. Chaldini's book on influence and persuasion. I know for me it was like waking up in the matrix if you remember that movie. It's one of the things where you know once you start seeing this stuff and you understand it and you see how everyone is using these principles in some way shape or form to influence us. You can't unsee it. Right, the challenges some people are using these for their benefit and not ours. So that's part of the risk. Great versus others are actually using these things for the betterment of us and the other side as well. So when interview people like Buffett business leaders, successful scientists, world leaders, all of them point to the same thing that you're at the home of influence. So welcome everyone tonight. Our goals we said this is an influence challenge. Okay. So we asked you and surveyed you. What are your influence challenges. What are you struggling with. Okay, what can we help with. And so we out of the hundreds of submissions that we got. We narrowed it down to five. We don't have the, you know, 29 hours or we looked at zooms max is 30 hours that we can be on here. We decided we can't do that but let's at least focus on five and see if we can get some good value to that should apply to everybody who made a submission. So tonight as you're going to see we're going to be talking about an international charity organization whose challenge was that they want to increase donor funding to their charity from public governments, private sector donors, etc. So how do you increase funding in an uncertain time when people tend to hang on to their money as a result. We're going to do a challenge where there's an international software company that would love to influence more sales by educating consumers about their other product offerings, meaning most people know them by one or two of their main product offerings, but they also would like the world and their existing customers to know Hey guys, we got a couple more things. How do we influence our folks and new folks to look at our other products as well. We had a our friend Derek Schwartz from the Texas State University submitted a challenge about his situation where he has a business, but he doesn't have a lot of time to dedicate to growing that business. But he still wants to grow it right as a transition plan or whatever. So how can he be and maximize the quality of his leads and sales opportunities because he doesn't have a lot of time in that business. We're going to learn about an Arizona based business group who have asked us how can they create messaging to increase the influence of Arizona's business leaders on policies among elected officials and voters in Arizona. Well, you know, we were talking to a group of people in this politician space, and the people in politics are spending something to the tune of like $18 billion just on this election alone. So is there a way that we can influence people and in politics and elected officials for the betterment of business owners in Arizona. We'll find out. And last but not least, Jeff King submitted a submission from Club Furniture. How can I influence sales of our company's online furniture products when people traditionally buy this type of furniture in person. Right. So how do we influence them to give up this tradition, so to speak, of touching it feeling it before they buy it to influence them to the online space. And to do that, and to answer all of these influence challenges we have none other than the godfather of influence himself, professor of Inridus of marketing and psychology at Arizona State University, world renowned influence and persuasion expert, Dr. Robert Chaldini. Thank you, Chris. I'm very glad to be with our audience today and want to begin by going through the five challenges straight away, because we've got a lot to cover. The first is the challenge regarding that international charity organization humanitarian organization that wants to increase their funding, getting more donors to contribute more to the good works of the organization. And how are you going to do that. Well, I'm going to suggest that they arrange for that by using the credibility that they have waiting for them in order to stand out from their from their customers from their competitors. So, how do you stand out. And, and one way is through the unparalleled the un un matchable power of credibility that exists in a person who is the credible authority, the person who has both knowledge and trustworthiness in the eyes of the audience. Well, how do you establish that. Well, I'm going to suggest something that most people have been trained not to do. And that is to mention a weakness in your appeal of your case, early in your presentation, most of the time we're told, oh, no, you keep your shortcomings, your weaknesses to the end if you mentioned them at all, because you want people leaning in your direction in all respects and at all times. It turns out that that's not the case. It turns out that if we mention a weakness in our case a little early, so somewhat early in our presentation, we establish the sense of credibility that works around the world. This is the case research in the Far East, in the Middle East, in Europe, in North America, in South America have always shown, if you as a communicator can arrange for your audience to see you as having both trustworthiness and knowledge on a topic. No one can beat you. All other things equal. No one can beat you. So how do you go about doing this. I'm going to suggest that you do it by mentioning, what just happened there, by mentioning a weakness, relatively early in your presentation, because what that tells people is first of all, you are knowledgeable, not just about the pros of a situation, but also the cons in that situation for your message, you're knowledgeable, but in addition, you're honest, you're trustworthy, you're willing to present the weaknesses as well as the strengths. Early on, because that establishes you as a credible source of information, and enriches the power of everything you say next. Now, when you present the strongest arguments in favor of your position, the most positive features of what you have to offer, people process that information more deeply, and they believe it more fully, because it's come from a credible source of information. Let me give you a couple of examples. Very famous examples from the charity, excuse me, the advertising community. A few years ago, now more than a few back in the 70s and 80s, Avis Rentakar had a campaign to try to combat their rival hurts, right, by saying, Avis, we're number two. They started out by saying that we're not number one, we're number two, but we try harder. The campaign produced a 700% increase in market share in one year. A more recent example of this is similar in that respect, or a L'Oreal, the cosmetics company had a campaign a few years ago, which began with a negative. And L'Oreal, we're expensive, but you're worth it. That campaign, triple market share in the course of one year. You can see why that would be powerful. Let's see how we can apply it to a charity organization like the one that submitted this challenge. And it has to do with something that occurred by... Sorry to interrupt you real quick. Sorry, the sharing was not working on your end, so I was just sharing my screen. We're just going to pause for one second while we attempt to get your screen sharing like it's supposed to. Okay. And while we're doing that, on that example, there was a good dental one that I remember from Listerine Mouth Rents. Listerine, the taste you hate three times a day. Exactly right. I remember that one well, which also went for years and years because it was so successful. So let's see how we can do this in a charity organization. This time, we'll go to a consultation that was done by my friend, my partner, my collaborator, Dr. Gregory Knightard. This was during 2008 to 2010 period of time when the US was in a recession and people weren't willing to spend their money on charities at the same degree. And he asked them, what do you say to people when you ask them for a contribution? And they said, here's what we say. The impact of your donation has never been greater than it is today. In other words, the positive side of it. But we know how difficult it is for many people to give during these difficult economic times. The weakness of their case. It's a difficult time. He asked them to simply reverse the sequence so that the new message was this. We know how difficult it is for many people to give during these difficult economic times. Something not favorable to their position, but they're going to admit it. And then they say, but the impact of your donation has never been greater than it is today. That small change produced a 36% increase in donations. Because they established themselves, think about this. They had knowledge. They know that these were difficult times. And they had the trustworthiness of being willing to admit it to people upfront. Something that is not likely to spur people to give, except that it established their trustworthiness and honesty and people and credibility and people gave. Now, this approach does more, by the way, than just establish credibility. It also serves as a differentiator for you and your message, because you don't have competitors who are standing out by doing this kind of thing. They're all presenting a blanket of positivity that they're trying to pull over people's eyes. Right. Not you. Not you. Okay, so that's one approach we can take. How about the next challenge that we can look to. Right. It's that software company, international software company. They were having problems because while they had plenty of customers who were buying their first or second most popular or most well known products. There were other products, very good products, and their customers weren't purchasing them. How could they go about moving people in the direction of these really valuable options that they had. I'm going to suggest that this organization use the principle of social proof. The one that says, if everybody is doing it, it must be the right thing. And when people are uncertain, they don't look inside for evidence of what they should do. They look outside. And one place they look is to the action of those around them, like them, their peers, their comparable others. Right. And we know that when they do that, if they see that there are two features of those others, that there are many of them that are moving in a particular direction. And they are similar to the audience, right, the people we're trying to convince. They say, oh, well, this is the right thing for me too then. Look at all these people, the many, and all these people who are similar to me, the comparable others to me. I can move in their direction and usually be right. Let's take a piece of evidence from a study that was done in McDonald's locations in South America, in Brazil and in Colombia. Researchers from IAW in London, our partner in London, Stephen J. Martin, did a study in which he asked the people at the desk to use one of two approaches to help selling desserts that most people didn't buy in McDonald's. McDonald's has desserts. They have a cookie, they have a fruit pie, and they have their McFlurry dish. Okay. And they were, people come in, they buy hamburgers and shakes and French fries and a soft drink, but they don't buy desserts. So what could they do? Well, here's what they usually say after you make your order. Would you like a dessert? We have a flurry, McFlurry, right? That was the control condition. Martin, Steve Martin, asked them to do one small thing differently for half of the customers to say, would you like a dessert? We have the McFlurry. It's our most popular dessert for the customers who come here. In other words, the many and the similar. Just like you. A lot of people who come here, just like you, choose the McFlurry dessert. Let me show you what happened to McFlurry sales as a result of simply saying, this is the favorite. 55% increase in McFlurry purchases. Now, it wasn't the dominant. It's like our software executives asked us, what if there are people who are not buying things that are good? All you have to do is tell them which of those options are your most popular, and they'll jump in. Because they're being steered correctly by the choices of other people around them like them. Now, let's add another component to this. Sometimes we have not just other products and services that we can provide. Sometimes they're add-ons to our most purchased products and services. So in this study, after people had decided to buy a McFlurry, they were told, oh, and by the way, our most popular topping, the add-on to the McFlurry. And then they described what that most popular topping was, depending on the location. Some places it was the chocolate sprinkle, some places it was the snicker pieces, some places it was peanuts and so on. Whatever it was, they said, this is our most popular, and the consequences were a 48% increase in add-on purchases. Once again, simply informing people of something that's true. All of this was true. We're steering people in the direction of something that's likely to be good for them and that they recognize is likely to be a choice for them, because a lot of people around them like them have chosen. So we can do that too. We can make that kind of commitment to honesty and credibility in our offers of the things that may not be so popular in one particular moment. Just tell them that they are the most popular of a particular category. Good. Let's move to the next principle that we can consider. And that are the next challenge. This is Derek's challenge. Remember, Derek in Texas, who didn't have a lot of time to work on his business because he had other things he was doing. He was at Texas State University. Perhaps he's a student. Perhaps he's, he works there as a faculty member or one of the employees of the university, didn't have time for his separate entrepreneurial business. So how could we help Derek? You might be in that situation as well. It doesn't involve urging Derek to work harder. He doesn't have time to work harder. What we have to do is urge him to work smarter. How do you work smarter without spending a lot of time in the process? There's very good research on this. It involves joining an entrepreneurial, in his case, an entrepreneurial group, a mastermind group, or getting a coach. Why? Why would that be successful? First of all, they don't take a lot of time. These groups, sometimes they meet once a week, sometimes twice a month, and so on. And you don't have to spend a lot of time. And what you do get is insights that you may not have thought about because you're dealing with people who have different perspectives, different backgrounds, different sets of experiences, and they can provide advice to you about something that you can change, something you can correct, something you can drop away from your approach and add something else. There's research to show, for example, that when it comes to problem-solving, groups outdistance a single person who's trying to solve that problem by him or herself. For these various reasons, they have a lot more experience and perspective to mine to offer insights that you might not get yourself. So that's certainly one reason. Here's another reason why these groups work so well. And finally, will you sharpen your thinking as a result? These groups, either the entrepreneurial groups or the mastermind groups, or even getting a coach, will require that you specify your goals and to make them public. Set them out, specify those goals, make them public so that you are accountable for reaching them. That accountability of a public commitment to a set of plans or a set of goals is a strong, powerful motivator for success. Here's a piece of evidence to suggest how powerful public goals are, setting out goals that are available to other people and that who can help you with the goals, but can also hold you accountable for them. There's a study done at a university with students who were having trouble in their first year of university. A lot of students had trouble in their first year. These students were on the verge of dropping out of the university because their grades were so low. And they enrolled in study groups, in which university professors gave them information on how to study better, how to use their time better, and so on. Some of the individuals were given this information, but not asked to set any goals. Other individuals were given this helpful information about how to study well and to manage their time, and they were asked to make goals and keep them in their heads. They were private goals set out for yourself, what it is that you want to achieve and how you're going to do so and keep that in your head. And then there was a final group, they were asked after receiving all this information to set those goals and plans and to announce them to everyone there in the group. What their goals would be. At the end of the year, the researchers looked at what percentage of each of these groups had raised their grades by a full letter grade, let's say from a D to a C or a C to a B or a B to a N a wherever it was in those particular courses, right, that they were taking. And then, hopefully, it was from a D to a C, but at least they're passing now, some of them from a C to a B and now they're starting to get really good at this process. Okay, so let's take a look at the percentage who made this kind of jump. Those who set no goal for themselves, only 14% of them had that kind of leap. The importance of setting a goal. Right, we want to be sure that those are those goals are specified. How about those people who specified those goals, but didn't make themselves accountable for them with anybody else in their group. 14, only 14% of them went to the next level. Right. That surprised me. But that was what the research showed. How about those people, however, who made those goals public. 86% of them went one full grade better in their courses. By the end of the year. Wow. A simple thing like that. So, what I'm going to suggest here is get into those groups. They provide feedback and they provide accountability. Oh, and there's one more thing about those groups that has shown to increase your performance if you get into them. It's not just that you win, because you get. Good advice from the people around you. You also perform better on your own tasks and issues and businesses. Because you give good advice. Research shows those people who give advice, then do better themselves. Why? The researchers have found out that it's because giving good advice enhances yourself confident. Confidence. You feel better about yourself as a business person, as somebody who can attack and resolve business problems, even if they're not your own. Right. And you consider yourself ready for anything that comes, even if something is not immediately on your forecast, but if it jumps in, you can offer good advice for that. And that increases your performance. So, that's where I think we could, we can move. Okay. Next challenge. This was the challenge, the challenge that we talked about from a business group that wanted to create messaging to increase the influence in Arizona where I live, to increase the influence of Arizona business leaders on the policies of elected officials and voters who help bring those elected officials into pop. So how can you move those people in the direction of this group? This was the question that our submitter had. And in this case, I'm going to recommend that we use the principle of unity to make this happen. Unity, it's, by the way, it's the seventh principle of influence. I started out with six, if you recall, started out with six and felt the need to add unity in the latest edition of my book. And it has to do with a sense of togetherness or weaness of people who share an identity in some kind of category, or an identity of shared values or shared goals, where they feel that they can say to others, those people are not just similar to us. They are of us. That person is one of us. And if you can arrange for people to see you as sharing a category like that, that that's an identity, a self identity category, those people will favor you and they will follow you. The research is very clear on this. You will get them to favor your choices and outcomes, wanting for you to do better, and they will follow your choices. They will see them as better if they see you as one of them. So what kind of categories exist like that? Certainly political parties. We're just seeing, we're just seeing political conventions these days. Religious denominations. Local regions, geographical regions. Oh, people from Philadelphia, people from the United States. And if you identify with those kinds of geographic categories, you want to help the people that you see as members with you in them. Also, people who share your values or share your goals, there's a sense of a weaness, a partnership with those people. Okay. So, how do we then bring weaness to top of mind for this particular challenge? I think that group of business executive leaders could emphasize commonality of place. We are all Arizonans after all. Right. They want to influence Arizona politicians, Arizona voters, and they are business leaders in Arizona. They want to emphasize that. That's one thing they should. Now, they, people know about the commonality, but they, you have to bring it to top of consciousness. You have to make it prominent in mind for it to operate successfully. The commonality of the socialization strategy has to emphasize that feature. Secondly, they share goals for the welfare of Arizonans. Right. We want everybody in our category to do well. You could do that by emphasizing how business leaders have contributed to the welfare of the state of Arizona by providing jobs, by contributing to Arizona based charities, by using corporate social responsibility activities within their organizations, and even supporting universities within the state. All of those things could be brought to the surface. Okay. Now, I'm going to suggest that our submitter for this challenge begin a campaign to make these points that will be labeled standing together for Arizona. Standing together where you are linked, unitized as in the same category, standing together for Arizona. And there's something very powerful about the image of standing together. Let me give you some evidence of it. There was a study done. It was done in Belgium, actually. Researchers brought subjects into an experiment where they showed the subjects various photographs of household items. Tables, chairs, picture frames, blenders, knives, forks, these kinds of things. They showed them these. And in the background of these, there were 20 different photographs. In the background, for one group of the subject, one sample of the subjects, there was an individual standing alone behind the item, just standing there. For another group of the subjects, another third of the subjects, there were two people standing in the background of each of the photos, but standing apart. And then finally, the final third of the subjects saw those same two individuals standing behind the items, household items, but shoulder to shoulder in a togetherness, we-ness pose. Just bringing that idea to consciousness in the minds of the people who saw those images. Now, here's what happened to all of the subjects in these experiments, in this experiment. The experimenter, a woman, said, okay, fine, we're done. Let me escort you to the door. Now we've completed this. And she got up and pretended to drop a series of items onto the floor, papers, pencils, pens, and so on, onto the floor. Here's the question. Who helps her? Who gets down off their chair onto their hands and knees to support this person after they've had one or another of these images put in their mind? Here's what we find. For those individuals who just saw somebody standing alone throughout the experiment behind those photographs in the background of those photographs, about 20% of them helped. Without being bitten, they weren't solicited. Oh, could you help me? They just did it spontaneously. They wanted to help. Okay, how about the people who saw two individuals not standing together, standing apart, separated from one another, where separation was the image, was the concept. 20% helped. Now, how about that third group? I'll bet you're suspecting that there was an increase among them. And you would be right, because with the image, with the concept of togetherness and unity in mind, right? And now they helped 60% of the time, got off their chairs spontaneously and helped because togetherness had been brought to consciousness. So, in that standing together campaign, emphasizing togetherness, right, having people standing shoulder to shoulder with other Arizonans, shoulder to shoulder, standing together in a group, that's going to be a watch, a very important watchword, togetherness in this group, in this campaign. Now, this difference between 20% and 60%, it's huge, right? That's a big difference, but it's not what surprised me the most about this study. What surprised me the most was something I had to read three times to be sure that I got it correct, because it was that the subjects in this experiment, including those who were three times more likely to go to the help, to the aid, to the assistance of somebody else, because togetherness was in their mind, were babies. They were 18 month old infants. And the concept of togetherness is so deeply embedded in us, the sense of unity of wanting to help when we feel a sense of unity, or have the concept at the top of mind, is so deeply embedded in us that it's there almost from the start. We'd be fools of the influence process if we didn't employ it in a way that allowed for everybody to gain as a consequence. Okay, one last challenge. This one from Jeff. Remember, Jeff has an online furniture company, and people are not buying as much of the good stuff. They're buying good furniture that he has at good prices, because they're not comfortable with an online purchase with that, with this model that's different from everything they're used to. Being able to try out a chair, a mattress or a table, whatever the item would be. How can he overcome this problem? I'm going to suggest that he try out the idea of pre-suasion, which opens the door to persuasion. Pre-suasion is the process of arranging for people to be sympathetic to a message before they encounter it. You might say, what? What is this? Some kind of magic? How can I get somebody to agree with a message before they know what's in it? Is this some kind of magic? No, it's not magic. It's solid behavioral science. For one reason, even though they don't know your message before you send it, you know your message. The crucial feature of your message that you want to put into people's minds before you send that message. Research shows if you can arrange for people to experience a concept of one sort or another that's related to the crux of your message before they ever encounter your message, they will have been ready for that concept. And they will be more attracted to it when they do encounter it. Here's a piece of evidence from an online furniture store that we can take a look at to make this point. There was a store that sold sofas online, right? And for one week, half of every visitor to their website on the landing page saw fluffy, soft clouds in the background. Before they encountered any other thing about the products or services and the various options, they saw clouds. Those people then rated comfort in their furniture purchases as significantly and more important, and they were more likely to purchase comfortable furniture. Compare them to another sample of visitors during that week. They were sent to a landing page that had small coins, pennies in the background. In other words, a cost-related concept, bringing the idea of price to consciousness. These people then rated price and cost as more important in their decision to buy, and they did purchase less expensive furniture. Okay, so this is evidence that what we put first before we send a message at all before people get any information, right? And then Jeff is going to steer them toward a concept that is in that earlier pre-poit, pre-sway-sive message that they will then encounter in our appeal that we think is so important for them to register. What would it be for Jeff in this case? For me, it seems to me that should be the concept of change. That's what he wants people to engage in, to move out of their old familiar approaches to something much more new and different. And what image is associated with change? In our minds, there's one that comes up at the top. It's a sunrise. It's a new day. Time to get out of our current state of affairs, sleep, whatever it is, and into a new day change. So here's what I'm going to suggest for Jeff's landing page. There should be three items. The first is the background of the landing page should have a sunrise in it. Secondly, before they receive any information about you, there's another thing that you can do on the landing page. And this isn't just for this particular challenge. We have a welcoming statement because it turns out that research on conversions of a website shows that if you have a welcoming statement on your landing page, you get significantly more conversions of visitors to buyers. Here's the one on my landing page, our landing page of the Chaldeany Institute. Welcome. I'm very pleased that you're considering one of my programs and then my signature. You welcome people the way you would welcome them into your home. That's what they want at the beginning. They want a welcoming statement to know that they're welcome there. That you're happy to see them there. They approve of you. They like you. They want you there. That statement significantly increases conversions. And then one final presuasive feature of your landing page. Or very soon after your landing page. It should be testimonials from satisfied customers who have great things to say about you and your products. And those especially from customers who had the same concerns that are keeping new customers from coming in your direction, but those were dispelled by the quality of your business model and and and products and services. Here's something else. There shouldn't be just one such testimonial. Make it first and make it multiply. Have more than one testimonial. That works better than a single one. Okay. Now, that kind of brings us to the end of our five challenges. I moved kind of quickly because we only have an hour, but we have plenty of information that we can provide to. To help you solve your influence challenges, not just the five that we considered with behavioral science. And I'm going to ask Dr. Phelps to give you some information about how you can go about getting some of that behavioral science information from the Chalini Institute if that's what you would wish. Thank you for your kind attention. Awesome. Thank you so much, Dr. Chalini. So much great information presented tonight. I hope everybody watching found as much value out of that as I did. Talking about credibility, how to start with a weakness in your case first, then pivoting on that magical word, but and then countering that negative with a positive showing them that you're transparent presenting the pros and the cons. Okay, allows you and creates more trust and credibility with that person in that moment. Leveraging the evidence of what many others are doing and what many similar others are doing. So if you have products or services that the majority of your customers are choosing, make sure to highlight those with your other existing customers who are not tapping into that, or new customers as well. Which we saw with the the goal setting study, right? So if you want to get your goals achieved, strike them off your list, so to speak, then make better commitments, right? Get a stronger commitment from yourself by getting in a group of others, making public commitments to them about what you're going to get done and how and then continue with accountability to meet with them to share if you were able to do that or not. Just that act alone of sharing them publicly with others automatically says this is that you're more likely to get those goals achieved. So whether it be a small mastermind group in your area or a group of other like minded entrepreneurs or business owners from around the country. Get up in groups, right? Let's check up together and leverage each other's commitments to push each other farther as a result. We saw the Arizona Unity example, leveraging this idea of standing together, right? What a powerful image and concept and campaign messaging that could be to help influence politicians and policymakers and voters in their area. And of course, I can't underestimate or underscore the value of persuasion. Getting a person, create a mindset in them before they come into that situation with you is key. So I love those examples with the pennies versus the furniture, but creating that mindset of the sunrise is powerful. Leveraging those testifiers, if you will, and putting that welcome statement on there is extremely powerful.
